source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: Alternative data has become table stakes for fundamental investors but is not a source of sustained competitive advantage for most funds.
summary_extended: Baker argues that alternative data — including credit card transaction data and sales tracking — has become commoditized and is now table stakes for fundamental investors rather than a source of durable competitive advantage. He reasons that because these datasets are widely available and priced on a scale basis, no single fund can maintain a proprietary edge over time; the notable exception is Renaissance Technologies, which he says locked up proprietary datasets under 50-to-100-year exclusive licenses. Baker further observes that the proliferation of alternative data is creating a structural market distortion: large earnings-season price moves are being driven by small, error-prone sample sets, generating mispricings that sophisticated investors can potentially exploit. The investment implication is that funds relying on alternative data as a core edge are likely to see that advantage erode, while the real opportunity may lie in identifying and fading the mispricings caused by over-reliance on noisy alternative data signals.
detail: Credit card data and sales tracking are now widely available and scale-priced, meaning no single fund has a true proprietary edge. The exception is Renaissance, which locked up proprietary datasets with 50-100 year licenses. Alternative data is causing large earnings-season price moves based on small, error-prone sample sets, creating mispricings.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this directly and emphatically as a structural observation about the industry.
quote_fragment: it is very difficult to get a sustained competitive advantage from alternative data
